data.title: Oil Price Volatility Is Our Base Case In A Visibly Surplus Market
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Massif Capital Q4 2025 Letter To Investors.

Oil and Natural Gas (16% of Portfolio, 3 Positions)

Oil price volatility in 2026 is our base case, and it appears less an aberration than a consequence of two forces that coexist uncomfortably: a visibly surplus market and an episodic geopolitical premium. On paper, the surplus is material, forecasts reach as high as 2.3 mb/d, yet prices remain hostage to recurrent geopolitical risk. Each recalibration of risk around the United States, Russia, Iran, or Venezuela temporarily lifts prices, even as the Oil Price and Geopolitical Events (2025 and 2026) underlying supply-and-demand arithmetic deteriorates. The pattern is familiar. In 2025, oil declined year over year, yet repeatedly spiked amid supply scares that proved transient.

What is less evident is a mechanism that would carry prices decisively higher or lower on an annual basis. Low OECD inventories and geopolitical fragility argue for a floor; rising non-OPEC supply via the arrival of the last wave of major post-COVID projects argues for a ceiling. Final investment decisions taken between 2020 and 2022, particularly offshore, are now converting into first oil across Brazil, Guyana, Norway, and the Gulf of Mexico. Unfortunately, there is scant evidence that oil majors have replenished the development queue with comparable new projects. The result is a market that likely clears excess barrels early in the year, then steadies as attention drifts toward 2027 and beyond when supply looks less robust.

Morgan Stanley (MS) frames the same outcome more mechanically. In The Oil Manual: Outlook 202c – Letting the Curve Do the Work, the bank argues that adjustment will occur through structure rather than level: rising inventories pressure the front of the Brent curve into contango while longer-dated prices remain comparatively anchored. Spot prices, in this telling, find support in the mid-$50s, not low enough to force capitulation, not high enough to revive enthusiasm. Volatility persists, direction does not.

Even geopolitical disruptions struggle to alter that oil trajectory sustainably. Severe outcomes in Iran or Venezuela would likely manifest as short squeezes rather than regime changes, their impact absorbed by an already ample surplus. By 2027, oil will have spent more than two years in retreat, importantly, not because demand failed to appear, but because supply overshot robust demand. That distinction matters. Prior sub-$60 episodes in the past decade, 2016 and 2018 to 2019, were resolved not by demand recovery but by investment withdrawal, a process that only reveals itself with a lag.

On that point, the numbers are unambiguous. The International Energy Agency estimates that, absent new investment, global oil production declines by roughly 5.5 mb/d annually, more than the combined output of Brazil and Norway. The US Energy Information Administration estimates demand growth of approximately 1.4 mb/d in 2026. The implication is a replacement requirement approaching 6.9 mb/d. Yet capital spending fell an estimated 5–6% last year, the first contraction since 2020. Those cuts do not bite immediately; they surface after a delay of 6 to 18 months. By the second half of 2026, the surplus that defines the year may already be eroding, quietly setting the conditions for a very different debate.

In this context, we believe our portfolio is well-positioned. If the market in 2026 is defined less by scarcity than by interruption, investment choices matter more than directional conviction. We therefore continue to favor companies whose economics are already proven at mid-cycle prices, whose capital has largely been spent, and whose balance sheets do not require the indulgence of a cooperative spot market. In a regime where Brent oscillates rather than trends, durability should command a premium over optionality.

Vår Energi (VARRY) is the most direct expression of this bias. Its cash flow profile is no longer prospective but operational, with the Johan Castberg and Balder X fields transitioning from promise to production. We estimate that in a $55 to $70 price range VAR has the highest free-cash-flow yield in the European E&P space, with a free-cash-flow yield of as much as 18%, a statistic that owes more to capex flexibility than to any heroic oil price assumption. The company’s ability to modulate spending while preserving distributions leaves it exposed to volatility, but not hostage to it.

Harbour Energy (PMOIF) complements this exposure in a different way. Harbour’s near-term uplift in free cash flow is driven less by oil prices than by balance-sheet and portfolio decisions, most notably the Waldorf and LLOG transactions, which transform the company’s cash generation across a $55–70/bbl Brent range. Under such conditions, we expect incremental free cash flow of $100–200 million in 2026, effectively doubling cumulative generation relative to the standalone base. In a surplus market punctuated by episodic geopolitical spikes, this distinction, managerial skill over commodity beta, matters.

Finally, Equinor (EQNR) provides ballast. With scale, diversification, and a net-cash balance sheet, Equinor’s exposure is skewed toward late-cycle offshore supply that has already cleared the sanctioning hurdle. Its familiarity with the Norwegian Continental Shelf and preference for infrastructure-led developments mean that near-term volumes are largely embedded in the forward-looking plan, reducing reliance on fresh final investment decisions at precisely the moment when the industry’s appetite for them appears to be waning. The common thread is that returns are being earned through past decisions rather than future oil price hopes. Should Morgan Stanley’s expectation of a contangoed curve, weak prompt pricing set against more resilient long-dated prices, materialize, and should volatility remain the dominant feature of 2026, the advantage should accrue to producers with high free-cash-flow yields, flexible capital programs, and balance-sheet slack. In such a market, the question is not who benefits most from a rally, but who can thrive in its absence.

Opinions expressed herein by Massif Capital, LLC (Massif Capital) are not an investment recommendation and are not meant to be relied upon in investment decisions. Massif Capital's opinions expressed herein address only select aspects of potential investment in securities of the companies mentioned and cannot be a substitute for comprehensive investment analysis. Any analysis presented herein is limited in scope, based on an incomplete set of information, and has limitations to its accuracy. Massif Capital recommends that potential and existing investors conduct thorough investment research of their own, including a detailed review of the companies' regulatory filings, public statements, and competitors. Consulting a qualified investment adviser may be prudent. The information upon which this material is based and was obtained from sources believed to be reliable but has not been independently verified. Therefore, Massif Capital cannot guarantee its accuracy. Any opinions or estimates constitute Massif Capital's best judgment as of the date of publication and are subject to change without notice. Massif Capital explicitly disclaims any liability that may arise from the use of this material; reliance upon information in this publication is at the sole discretion of the reader. Furthermore, under no circumstances is this publication an offer to sell or a solicitation to buy securities or services discussed herein.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PPA: This Defense ETF's Massive 2025 Outperformance Could Continue
data.description:
After delivering one of the best returns in 2025 and beating the S&P 500 by 125%, Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF’s (PPA) share price surge accelerated in 2026 due to the global geopolitical tensions and higher defense spending. In addition, the defense companies backlog and financial outlook also indicate a robust and long-lasting growth trend ahead. Therefore, it is still a great time to invest in the defense industry through investment vehicles, such as Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF (PPA). I maintain my buy rating on PPA.

PPA’s 2025 Performance

Fiscal 2025 was one of the best years for defense investors, supported by the global geopolitical tensions. In addition, a global approach of equipping armies with the latest defense technologies boosted the demand trend. Over the past few years, the technological innovations have changed war strategies. Instead of gathering large armies, countries are now relying on unmanned drones, jets, missiles and other technologies for accuracy.

The growth trend has also been reflecting in defense-focused securities performance. The Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF, which tracks the performance of companies systemically important to defense and security, delivered 36% in price return in 2025 compared the S&P 500’s 16% upside. In 2024 and 2023, PPA’s price returns were 24% and 18%, respectively.

The Potential Upside in 2026

PPA’s price momentum is likely to accelerate in 2026 due to the prospects of a further boost in defense spending across the world. In particular, United States, the largest defense spender, suggested 2027 military budget around $1.5 trillion, significantly higher compared to $901 billion approved for 2026.

The escalating tensions between US and Iran, increasing stress among Saudi Arabia and UAE and ongoing Russia-Ukraine conflict contributes to defense demand. In addition, tensions between Israel and Middle Eastern countries and challenging geopolitical conditions in the Asia Pacific region have also been adding to an unprecedented demand. European region also seeks to steadily reach their defense spending target of 5% of GDP.

Overall, the defense segment’s fundamentals seem significantly strong. Therefore, despite a blistering share prices growth in the past few years, it still appears to be a great time to invest in the industry through investment vehicles, such as Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF. Year to date, PPA's share price surged 15%. I expect PPA to deliver higher returns in 2026 compared to 2025.

PPA’s Portfolio Holdings Indicate Robust and Long-Lasting Growth

Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF tracks SPADE Defense Index by using full replication technique. PPA's portfolio comprises 62 defense stocks. The fund’s top 10 holdings make-up 59% of its portfolio weight. Boeing (BA), RTX (RTX), Lockheed Martin (LMT), GE Aerospace (GE) and Northrop Grumman Corp (NOC) are its top five holdings. All these companies generated a blistering growth in the last twelve months amid their record profitability and massive backlogs.

For example, Boeing's share price rallied 14% year to date and 46% in the last twelve months. In the latest quarter, the company’s backlog grew to $636 billion. Whereas, RTX Corp experienced sales growth of 12% and its adjusted earnings per share increased 17% compared to the prior year. It ended the quarter with a backlog of $251 billion, representing 13% increase year-over-year. The company’s share price soared 10% year to date and 67% in the last twelve months.

Similarly, GE Aerospace, Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman reported an unprecedented demand trend. Lockheed Martin, the fighter jet maker, anticipates that its record $179 billion backlog is equivalent to more than two and a half years of its sales. Therefore, the company seeks to increase its operational efficiencies to meet the demand trend. Here is what its CEO stated:

Based on the effectiveness and reliability of our products and systems, strong demand from Lockheed Martin's customers—both in the United States and among our allies—continues. As a result of this unprecedented demand, we are increasing production capacity significantly across a wide range of our lines of business

PPA’s next five holdings are General Dynamics (GD), L3Harris Technologies (LHX), Honeywell International (HON), Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and Parker Hannifin (PH). All these companies have also delivered solid share price growth on the back of robust demand trend.

In the latest quarter, General Dynamics reported 10.6% revenue and 15.8% earnings per share growth from the year-ago quarter. The company claims its order activity was very strong as its total estimated contract value reached $167.7 billion. Howmet Aerospace, which generated 14% revenue and 17% earning growth in the latest quarter, anticipates strong commercial and defense demand in 2026.

PPA’s Interesting Characteristics

Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF’s other key characteristics include a solid liquidity. PPA’s average shares trading volume hovers around 200K, up from the median of all ETFs of 37K. Its assets under management are around $8 billion. The fund also lifted its dividend in the last two consecutive years. In 2026, a hefty dividend hike is expected, given the robust earnings growth power of its portfolio holdings. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.58% is slightly higher from the median of all ETFs of 0.50%.

Other Players Also Offer Lofty Returns

Other popular options in the aerospace & defense industry include iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA), State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR) and Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD). All three ETFs generated a blistering growth in the last twelve months. In particular, SHLD’s price rally of nearly 100% indicates the robust demand for defense technology. The fund seeks to track the performance of companies with a greater exposure to defense technologies.

Whereas, iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF mainly targets US based aerospace & defense companies. It seeks to track the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Select Aerospace & Defense Index by using representative sampling technique. Its portfolio comprises 46 stocks. On the other hand, State Street® SPDR® S&P® Aerospace & Defense ETF offers a more targeted exposure to the US aerospace & defense industry. Its portfolio is make-up of 41 stocks.

Risk Factors

The aerospace & defense industry is exposed to various risk factors. The risk of tariffs, regulatory challenges and economic trends can negatively impact its performance. As the United States is the largest exporter of defense & aerospace products, the higher tariffs and regulatory challenges can impact the industry’s performance. In addition, a recession can also create challenges for the industry.

Final Thoughts

As demand in defense category is expected to accelerate in 2026, it is still a great time to capitalize on potential opportunities. Invesco Aerospace & Defense ETF is one of the best options with exposure to defense category. Its portfolio comprises a large number of holdings. The fund’s price momentum is robust, liquidity is strong and dividend growth is healthy. Whereas, the defense technology investors can also consider ETFs like SHLD. ITA and XAR could also be intersting options to gain a targeted exposure to US aerospace & defense industry.

data.title: Wolverine World Wide: Shares Are Getting Close To Being On Sale
data.description:
Honestly, I have never been much of a big fan of the clothing and apparel market. It is highly competitive, low margin, and subject to fickle consumer preferences. But there has always been one segment in this market that has appealed to me. And that is none other than the footwear space. Some of the companies in this market are attractive in doing exceptionally well. Others are doing fine, but not great. And even the ones that are doing fine don't necessarily make for attractive opportunities. One firm that I have been more neutral on as of late is Wolverine World Wide, Inc. (WWW), an enterprise that focuses on casual footwear and apparel, performance outdoor and athletic footwear and apparel, kids’ footwear, industrial work boots and apparel, and uniform shoes and boots. As of the end of 2024, the company generated sales from roughly 170 countries and territories. This makes it a truly global player. In addition to owning some of its own brands, it also licenses brands.

This is the kind of candidate that I would typically be drawn to. But in late October of last year, even as financial performance was coming in strong, I reaffirmed the business as a ‘hold’ candidate. This was driven not only by valuation, but by other factors as well, such as the risk that tariffs posed, the absence of detailed guidance from management, and broader economic uncertainty. Since then, the stock has dropped, falling 16.3% while the S&P 500 is up 1.4%. And even though that has been painful, I am honestly getting closer to upgrading the stock. Relative to other similar firms, it is a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. And if we see a further decline, an upgrade will be unavoidable absent a big change in fundamentals. For now, however, I believe that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ is the right choice.

Not A Good Fit… Yet

The only new data that investors have regarding Wolverine World Wide covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. That window of time saw revenue come in strong at $470.3 million. That's an increase of 6.8% over the $440.2 million the company reported a year earlier. That rise in revenue brought with it a slight increase in profitability, with net income climbing from $23.2 million to $25.1 million. Other profitability metrics were mixed. For instance, operating cash flow fell precipitously from $108.2 million to $33 million. Even if we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $71 million to $37.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business did increase, growing from $33.2 million to $48.1 million.

The first nine months of 2025 saw similar mixed performance. However, on the whole, I would say that window of time was better than the third quarter on its own. Revenue, profits, and two of the three cash flow metrics for the company, all improved year over year. The only thing that worsened was operating cash flow. To really understand all that is going on, however, we would be best off digging into each of the operating segments that the company has. Although there is a small ‘other’ segment that accounts for a small portion of revenue, broadly speaking, there are only two operating segments that really have a big impact on the business.

The most significant of these is the Active Group, which focuses on its Merrell footwear and apparel, Saucony footwear and apparel, Sweaty Betty activewear, and its Chaco footwear products. According to management, revenue for this segment amounted to $352.8 million for the third quarter of 2025. That's 10.7% above the $318.7 million the segment was responsible for the previous year. This expansion is multifaceted, with some of it being brand-specific and some of it being driven by growth in key markets. Specifically, for instance, the company has talked about strong sales growth in the US and EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions.

The firm is also actively investing in launching new product lines. One example of this would be the ProGrid Omni 9 while another is Ride Millennium. This does not mean that there isn't some weakness. For instance, Sweaty Betty reported a decline in revenue because of soft consumer demand in the US direct to consumer and wholesale channels. Lower close out sales also proved to be a problem. And on top of this, a reduction in close out sales and soft consumer demand negatively affected revenue for its Chaco brand line. Fortunately, the rise in revenue here brought with it higher profits. Operating income grew from $53.3 million to $65.4 million.

By comparison, the Work Group hasn't fared as well. This involves its famous Wolverine footwear and apparel brand, as well as others such as Cat, Bates, Harley-Davidson, and HYTEST. Revenue for this segment actually dropped from $109.1 million to $105.9 million year over year. This doesn't actually surprise me. The economic climate in the US is worsening, which wouldn't necessarily lead to a reduction in demand when it comes to work related footwear and apparel. And on top of this, the company was negatively impacted to the tune of $1.3 million from the Harley-Davidson brand, which as I have detailed in prior articles is experiencing challenges because younger consumers prefer smaller and more affordable bikes. The good news is that, even though revenue worsened, profitability improved, growing from $16.3 million to $19.8 million. Management chalked this up to a higher mix of full price sales, a reduction in promotional activity, price increases that the company implemented, and supply chain cost initiatives that the business has been pushing for. It does appear as though certain tariff mitigation strategies are helping. So that's great to see.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year as a whole, management believes that revenue will have totaled between $1.855 billion and $1.87 billion. That would compare to the $1.76 billion the company reported for 2024. They also expect net profits of between $105 million and $109 million. For context, in the previous year, net income was only $47.9 million. But to be fair, profitability has long been volatile for this prospect. If we project out results based on what the company has seen already for 2025, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of about $138.1 million and EBITDA of $188.9 million. Those would both be improvements over what was seen in the previous year.

Armed with these estimates, and also using the results of the company achieved for 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. Honestly, these multiples aren't bad. They just aren't great, either. Relative to other similar firms, it is worth noting that the stock is a tad bit on the cheap end of the spectrum. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. On a price to earnings basis, only one of the five institutions that I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And two of them were cheaper when it came to the other valuation approaches. This means that, relatively speaking, the company is looking decent. But as a value investor who is looking to outperform the market, I prefer a larger discount than what we see here, especially in light of recent mixed bottom-line results.

The good news is that management is working hard to capture additional market share. This includes pushing its Merrell and Saucony brand lines that the company described as performing strongly. They specifically mentioned in their third quarter earnings call that they are capturing market share here. What's really exciting to me is Saucony, which the company said is experiencing door count penetration of less than 25% of what it believes to be its potential. They expect the first half of 2026 to see greater performance on this basis and what we've seen in the first half of 2025. This does indicate that 2026 is likely to see some strength for the business relative to 2024. And if the stock price remains depressed, while fundamentals improve, that could certainly change my assessment of the picture.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I would argue that Wolverine World Wide is certainly worthy of attention. It's not a company that I feel comfortable upgrading just yet. But it's getting awfully close. If we do see some continued improvements from a fundamental standpoint, and/or we see the stock price fall further, an upgrade could be in store. But for now, I think that maintaining it as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right choice.

data.title: Hilton Worldwide Holdings: The Bull Case Remains Compelling
data.description:
Shares of Hilton Worldwide Holdings (HLT) have delivered a total return of 27% since my previous piece, Hilton Worldwide: 5 Reasons Why The Stock Is Now A Strong Buy, was published on March 11, 2025.

The move higher in Hilton shares has been driven by solid operating performance resulting in strong EPS growth. Additionally, the stock has experienced modest valuation multiple expansion. I believe the company's growth outlook remains strong and valuation remains attractive. Additionally, I view the company's business as being fairly insulated from AI disruption and Trump disruption risks. For these reasons, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock.

Strong Operating Performance Despite Macro Challenges

The operating environment for the hotel business has been somewhat challenging of late. In the time since my previous piece, significant tariffs have come into force in the U.S., the U.S. government experienced its longest shutdown in history, and international travel to the U.S. declined in 2025. Despite these challenges, Hilton has still been able to deliver solid operating performance. In Q3 2025, Hilton noted that it expects to deliver FY 2025 system-wide RevPAR growth of 0% to 1%, which represents only a slight decline from the company's long-term target of 2%-4% annual RevPAR growth. Moreover, the company also continues to execute on its new room growth plans. Q3 2025 net unit growth came in at 6.5% relative to the same period a year ago, which is in line with the company's long-term new unit growth target.

As shown by the chart below, despite significant macro-driven challenges, consensus EPS estimates for FY 2025 have increased slightly from the level at the time of my previous piece. The stability of the company's earnings power speaks to the strength of its business model and focus on using new unit growth to drive EPS growth. At the time of my previous piece, Hilton had a development pipeline of 499,000 rooms, which has now increased to 515,000 rooms.

2026 Outlook

While the company has not yet released FY 2026 guidance, management noted on the company's Q3 calls that it feels incrementally better about the setup for 2026 compared to 2025. I agree with this view and expect business conditions in the U.S. to improve significantly relative to 2025. Consensus estimates currently call for the company to deliver FY 2026 EPS of $9.14, which represents 13.2% growth relative to the current consensus FY 2025 EPS estimate of $8.07.

I view the 13.2% EPS growth estimate for FY 2026 as reasonable given the company's continued momentum in its development of new units. New unit growth should allow Hilton to drive 6%-7% EPS growth, while a return to a more normal U.S. travel environment should help RevPAR growth come in closer to the company's 2%-4% long-term target. A third important driver of EPS growth is the company's share repurchase program. On January 14, 2026, Hilton announced that its Board had approved an increase of $3.5 billion to the company's share repurchase program, bringing the total authorization to $4.6 billion. Hilton currently has a market cap of $70 billion, and thus the repurchase authorization represents roughly 6.5% of shares outstanding. A pace of repurchases similar to recent history would suggest Hilton could complete more than $3.2 billion of purchases in FY 2026, which could drive an incremental 4.5% increase in EPS.

Taken together, assuming a 4.5% benefit from share repurchases, 6.5% new unit growth, and RevPAR of 3%, it would result in annual EPS growth of 14%, which is slightly better than consensus estimates. Additionally, the company should be able to drive incremental additional EPS growth through increasing franchise fees as contracts reset and the 5% in-place rate remains below the 5.6% steady-state rate. For these reasons, I believe Hilton is in a strong position to meet or exceed consensus EPS estimates for FY 2026.

Valuation Update

Hilton shares currently trade at 33x consensus FY 2026 EPS. Comparably, at the time of my previous piece, Hilton had been trading at 31x consensus forward EPS. Thus, Hilton has experienced modest valuation multiple expansion since the time of my previous piece. However, it is important to note that the broader market has also experienced modest valuation multiple expansion over the same time period. At the time of my prior piece, the S&P 500 had been trading at 21x consensus forward earnings, and it now trades at 22.3x consensus forward earnings. Thus, on a relative basis, Hilton has only experienced a very small valuation premium increase relative to the broader market. I continue to believe that Hilton has better long-term growth prospects (as I see potential for 13%+ EPS growth for the next decade), while I expect the S&P 500 to revert back to historical norms of high single-digit EPS growth in the coming years (following above-trend growth in 2026).

Hilton continues to trade at a moderate premium relative to its own historical average forward P/E multiple. This was the case at the time of my prior piece, and I continue to believe this premium is justified given the company's strong pipeline and increasingly resilient and geographically diversified earnings power.

Hilton shares continue to trade at a modest premium to peers Marriott and IHG. Hilton's valuation premium relative to these two peers has remained fairly stable since the time of my previous piece, and I continue to view Hilton's premium as reasonable given its stronger medium-term growth prospects. For context, Hilton's development pipeline share is 3.7x its existing share, while the metric is 2.5x for Marriott and 3x for IHG. Hyatt has better near-term EPS growth prospects and continues to trade at a substantial premium to Hilton.

Overall, I continue to view Hilton's valuation as reasonable and do not expect a sharp drop in the stock's forward P/E. That said, I do see more limited scope for valuation multiple expansion now than was the case at the time of my prior piece. The reason for this is that Hilton's multiple has already experienced modest multiple expansion relative to where the stock was trading at the time of my previous piece.

Insulated From AI And Trump Risks

The risks related to disruption from AI have spread to an increasingly wide range of companies. One sector that has come under significant selling pressure in recent months is the software sector amid investor concern that AI could pose a serious risk to these companies given the potential for decreased user count for software licenses. Another company that has seen a negative impact related to AI risk is Netflix, as AI has the potential to result in much lower content production costs, which could erode the company's moat due to its massive content spending advantage relative to peers. In terms of the travel industry, shares of Booking Holdings have recently been under pressure amid worries that AI could increase disintermediation risk as AI agents would be able to book directly with hotels and airlines. I see relatively limited AI risk to Hilton's business model and view the company as a potential beneficiary. One reason for this is that AI, in the long run, has the potential to make Hilton more efficient, resulting in a reduction of operating expenses and potential margin expansion. Moreover, to the extent AI platforms result in some disintermediation of online travel agencies (“OTAs”), Hilton could benefit due to incrementally better pricing due to more direct bookings.

President Trump's drive to improve affordability has impacted an increasing number of companies. Impacted companies include drug companies that have been forced to agree to significant pricing concessions, banks and credit card companies that have the potential to face significant headwinds related to Trump's drive to cap credit card interest at 10%, and independent power producers who stand to lose if Trump's desired cap on how much they can charge comes into effect. I believe that Trump's affordability push could expand to other sectors as well. However, I believe the hotel business is more immune to Trump-related affordability push risks, as travel tends to be a more discretionary expense and lower profile in nature as it relates to the affordability discussion. Moreover, the market is very diverse, with prices being set in each individual market. Additionally, I believe Hilton's risk related to a potential increase in tariffs is also fairly limited as the company's business is not based on physical goods. Given these factors, I believe that Hilton is more immune to Trump risk relative to many other sectors, which should be a positive for the stock in the near term.

Risks To Consider

The main near-term risk to the Hilton bull case would be a prolonged economic downturn. Travel tends to be highly discretionary in nature, and in the event of a sharp economic contraction, RevPAR would likely fall significantly for Hilton. Additionally, I would expect new unit growth and pipeline growth to slow in the event of a prolonged economic downturn. I do not expect an economic downturn in the near term and believe that lower interest rates in the U.S. could fuel a stronger-than-expected economy in the near term. That said, I would view any significant sell-off in Hilton shares related to macroeconomic risks as a long-term buying opportunity.

Conclusion

Hilton shares have delivered strong results since my previous Strong Buy piece was published. The company has delivered solid earnings growth despite a challenging macro backdrop. I expect the company to deliver another year of strong earnings growth in 2026, which has the potential to drive above-market returns for the stock.

Hilton shares have experienced modest valuation multiple expansion since the time of my prior piece, but I continue to view the stock's valuation as attractive relative to the broader market given strong long-term growth prospects. Additionally, I view the company as being fairly insulated from AI and Trump-related risks. For these reasons, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating on the stock and believe that the bull case remains compelling.

data.title: XRP Treasury Play: Armada Acquisition Corp. II
data.description:
Armada Acquisition Corp. II (XRPN) is a SPAC merger with Evernorth, a digital asset treasury (“DAT”) focused on XRP (XRP-USD). While DATs and SPAC mergers often are fraught with problems, this opportunity seems pretty conservative and is worth consideration.

Proposed SPAC Merger

Back in October, Evernorth announced the proposed merger, and Armada II's ticker changed to XRPN a few weeks later. This does not mean that the deal has closed, however, and the announcement indicated that it was expected to do so in Q1 2026.

Other details included:

  • Over $1 billion in gross proceeds
  • About $230M is from the SPAC's trust
  • At least $800M in a PIPE offering
  • Valuation of about $1.4B

Evernorth is currently getting the transaction approved with the SEC. At that point, more thorough disclosures will be available about their underlying financials, so much of this thesis relies on estimates.

Evernorth's Business

Evernorth already operates a DAT with a dedicated focus on XRP, the native currency of Ripple's blockchain network. As of November, they declared ownership of 84.4M XRP at an average price of $2.54.

XRP has been selected by Evernorth because it believes Ripple's blockchain network supports faster and cheaper transactions than those of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD).

XRP is also one of the largest cryptos by market cap, ranking fifth globally overall. This makes it one of the more relevant alternatives around which to form a DAT. Where it is different, however, is in its finite supply, which was fully capped when the coin was launched at 100B XRP. This means that the supply does not grow from processes such as mining or staking.

When I covered DeFi Development Corp. (DFDV), I highlighted its ability to use Solana (SOL-USD) staking as the basis for an operating business to produce cash flow, something that I found to be tragically missing in Strategy's (MSTR) approach as a DAT. Even though XRP does not allow for staking in the same way, that doesn't mean it lacks an operating business.

Evernorth generates revenue by operating a bit more like an old-school financial by providing liquidity, mainly through:

  • Loans denominated in XRP, yielding interest
  • Market making XRP, creating a spread, and earning trading fees

By doing these on-chain or in structured deals with financial institutions, Evernorth aims to be a bridge between DeFi and TradFi through its utilization of XRP.

In effect, total XRP holdings produce some kind of yield as their revenue. Fully disclosed financials are not available yet, and estimates range from 5% to 10%.

Yield from operations supports XRP accumulation. Of course, they can raise capital to support more accumulation over time, just like other DATs, particularly if shares trade at a premium to NAV or XRP per share.

Outlook and Valuation

Evernorth may be a bit lucky in that their merger and capital raise are occurring after the bearishness that has plagued cryptocurrencies since the fall.

After President Trump announced more tariffs on China on Oct. 10th, crypto prices began to decline and have largely not recovered. Many DATs that raised capital and purchased their coins at the peak of the euphoria in July (following the passage of the GENIUS Act) have had a tough time. Many trade at discounts to NAV and cannot accretively raise capital to purchase more.

As most of their XRP will be purchased after this, they will be in a better position to grow the pot and perhaps ride any market recovery. Based on XRP's recent prices ($2.00 to $2.10), total holdings could be worth about $2B. Yielding 5% to 10% from the various ways they deploy XRP, this suggests revenue of $100M to $200M.

As XRP's daily volume is close to the holdings Evernorth would have, it's feasible that minimal trading fees (0.01% to 0.02%) could support the lower potential yield. Loans with institutions could potentially be structured to yield more and pull the total up.

DATs don't tend to have a lot of overhead, so there's a good chance most of this survives into net income (which would work out as retained XRP). A breakdown of their expenses will make this clearer.

Even without raising capital, it's a very accretive opportunity. Plus, the SPAC shares come with the standard redemption rights. This is $10 per share for XRPN's purposes. As the stock barely trades above that, nearly all principal is safe for now, with potential upside from the merger and deployment of the XRP holdings to follow.

Risk Factors

A few things can complicate this thesis. Bearishness in crypto markets, to include XRP, could persist. This could happen after they accumulate their XRP holdings from the capital raised. This would make it hard to raise capital and accumulate more, delaying their strategy.

We also don't yet know if management is willing to issue any kind of fixed-income securities, such as bonds or preferred stock.

As MSTR's six-month performance has shown, the impacts of these are painful on the downside and require dilution of the common to meet fixed income obligations.

Lastly, while there's natural compounding here, Evernorth is still limited by demand. Only so many people want XRP-denominated loans right now, and only so much trading volume will occur each day. Demand ceilings limit yield, and if expenses aren't managed, this could challenge profitability.

Conclusion

Investors have the opportunity to get into this DAT at roughly the lowest possible entry price, given the redemption rights of SPAC shares. Evernorth, as the merger candidate, isn't just doing capital raises and accumulation; it has an operating business to compound that should support use of its target crypto, XRP, over time.

Overall, I see reasonable upside for both the short and long terms with little upfront risk, and so I initiate coverage with a Buy.

data.title: lululemon athletica: Activist Interest Reinforces A Mispriced Turnaround Story
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

lululemon athletica (LULU) is down nearly 50% over the past year, trading at some of the lowest levels in over half a decade, fueled by rising internal and macro uncertainty, which I believe offers a solid opportunity to get exposure to what could end up being a very strong turnaround, which recently sparked the attention of legendary names in the market.

Reaching the end of their 5-year cycle soon, LULU outlined $11 billion in revenue for 2025 (nearly tripling during this cycle despite the ongoing environment) during their Q3 Earnings Call, with significant growth internationally despite the domestic weakness, fueled by China and their digital expansion.

Recently, thanks to a strong holiday season, LULU even put out a statement saying that they now expect the net revenue and EPS to be toward the high end of the previously guided $3.5 billion to $3.585 billion and $4.66 to $4.76, respectively.

Although the free cash flow has been negative over 9M'25 so far due to significant working capital hits combined with an increase in CAPEX, we should note the significant seasonality (see below), with Q4 often coming with very significant operating cash flows.

As we can see, over half of 2024's cash flow from operating activities came in the last quarter, with the free cash flow reaching $1.58 billion, $1.64 billion in 2024 and 2023, while 2022 was hit by their significant inventory build-up and other working capital issues.

Still, even if we assume a decrease to $1.5 billion or even $1.4 billion as a result of the higher costs, tariffs, and bigger CAPEX, that's still not bad at all for a ~$23.68 billion market cap, especially from a high-quality business with strong growth potential.

Financially, based on LULU's latest report, we can see a very solid position, with the current assets alone more than covering their total liabilities, with over $1 billion in cash and equivalents (and ~$2 billion in inventory) that places them in a solid position to fund their international expansion.

If they want to, there is plenty of room to fuel this through debt, especially if they tap into international markets and manage to get it for lower yields.

As of their Q3'25 report, LULU has repurchased ~$906.58 million worth of stock during 9M'25, which is unfortunately a decrease compared to 9M'24, when the stock was significantly more expensive. Still, on December 3, 2025, LULU's board authorized a $1 billion increase to their buybacks, bringing the remaining authorization to $1.6 billion now, and I'd argue that this is a solid time for accelerated buybacks, especially since they can afford them.

Risks & Opportunities

Although debt costs aren't that important for LULU directly since they have a meaningless amount, they can still affect their consumers, partners, and especially the market's expectations, as they are tied to the risk-free rate that's ultimately impacting valuations.

I believe it's important to mention that we're not necessarily talking about a company-specific issue (Nike (NKE) reported stagnating numbers for a few years now) or the argument that LULU has been just a fad that's now fading.

The recent 33% growth in international revenue is a good argument against the latter, while looking at many other companies (even well beyond just apparel), we can see that we're actually talking about country-wide issues, as even food companies that I've covered recently are reporting significant slowdowns in volumes, with a general de-premiumization trend and accentuated K-shaped economic developments.

Countless recent studies showed the weakness and stress reported by consumers, as they are facing rising costs of living, lower income, job insecurity, etc., and LULU is far from immune to that, having to maintain/grow their brand while also facing competitive pressure from industry titans on top.

This has been going on especially since the rate hikes, but the tariff uncertainty adds another layer to it, while the uncertainty around the Fed's independence and its next moves can lead to even more pressure. To that, add all the rising geopolitical uncertainty (Greenland, Iran, Venezuela), renewed tariff uncertainty as Trump just threatened that he "may punish countries with tariffs" if they don't agree with his Greenland plans, and other factors that can lead to even less confidence from the market.

As a result, the market can push debt costs higher, which is its way of adjusting the reward to the new risks and uncertainty it has to take compared to the "safe haven" that US treasuries used to be, while the environment continues to remain uncertain, with hard, safe haven assets like gold or silver reaching new all-time highs almost on a daily basis nowadays. Again, it's not as important for LULU from a balance sheet perspective, but it can influence their growth and valuation.

Still, given the high quality and brand popularity combined with the massive expansion potential, I wouldn't be surprised to see a giant like LVMH (LVMHF) try to buy LULU at some point if this pressure continues.

These kinds of acquisitions are at the core of industry titans like LVMH, who can use their premium valuations to buy strong brands and develop them considerably using their global supply chain and know-how, which can arguably unlock significant value in LULU's international expansion and potentially be very accretive for LVMH, especially if they buy them at a distressed valuation.

Meanwhile, the recent pressure from their founder and even a recent $1 billion position from Elliott Investment Management can also lead to positive changes, and the market seems to believe that despite growing above peers recently, LULU could've achieved more than a 7% increase in revenue if they had better leadership.

The CEO is already stepping down (resignation effective at the end of January 2026), and this is generally the kind of position where Elliott plays very well, allowing them to even reshape the company's future thanks to the kind of influence they have, already viewing the former CFO and COO of Ralph Lauren (RL), Jane Nielsen - where she had an "immense success"—as a strong candidate.

In other words, right now, we're looking at a ton of interest not only from an industry veteran that built one of the biggest apparel brands, but also one of the most popular activist investor funds, and I believe that should say something about the company's potential.

Meanwhile, LULU has recently announced their plans to continue scaling their international expansion, preparing to enter Greece, Austria, Poland, Hungary, and Romania in 2026 in addition to the previously announced entry into India.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the strong financial position and significant international expansion potential, LULU is still ultimately limited by the consumer environment, especially since we're talking about a high-quality brand that can be pressured by de-premiumization trends.

Meanwhile, we should keep in mind that an international expansion can also fail, especially if they get "lost" in the management/leadership changing process, while their domestic popularity can fade as competition intensifies, with industry giants potentially taking advantage of any missteps.

Valuation

For a Lululemon's FCF to Equity valuation, let's begin with $1.4 billion in free cash flow in 2025, which is below the past couple of years and takes into account macro pressures, followed by a 7.5% CAGR thanks to low single-digit growth in the US combined with double-digit international expansion, followed by a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a 10% discount rate to take into account the higher risks compared to the industry's giants and 118.55 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $24.71 billion, for an intrinsic value of $208.46 per share, which is above the current $201.87 at the moment of writing this.

Even this relatively conservative valuation (low starting FCF, much lower CAGR than recent history even during a weak environment, higher discount rate) leads to a price that's slightly above the current level, while there is plenty of potential for them to do much better thanks to tariffs, improving consumer environment, and their international expansion.

Conclusion

Overall, Lululemon is a buy in my opinion, with strong cash flow, a healthy balance sheet, high-quality products, and significant potential thanks to their international expansion.

There are definitely risks, although the market seems to be accounting for quite a weak future already, as if they were already in a much more mature stage, but the industry's recovery combined with all the pressure on the board and leadership could lead to a very profitable turnaround story.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TPZ: Why Energy Investors Should Not Only Chase Dividends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tortoise Electrification Infrastructure ETF (NYSE:TPZ) is displaying concerning signals for its top holdings including significant leverage, low growth, and other risk factors. While the fund offers a solid dividend yield, investors should not overlook these factors along with a high expense ratio that will erode returns. As a result, the fund warrants a sell rating, particularly for investors that are seeking a low-cost fund for capital appreciation in addition to income.

Tortoise Electrification Infrastructure ETF – Overview and Compared ETFs

TPZ is an actively managed ETF that seeks to track energy and electrification companies with the objective of monthly income. The fund accomplishes this objective by capturing dividend-paying companies and using an options strategy. With its inception in 2024, the fund has 33 holdings and $131M in AUM. TPZ includes 64% weight on power infrastructure along with 30% weight on natural gas infrastructure and 7% weight on liquids infrastructure.

For comparison purposes, other funds examined are First Trust NASDAQ Clean Edge Smart Grid Infrastructure Index Fund (GRID), Tema Electrification ETF (VOLT), and iShares Global Infrastructure Fund (IGF). GRID seeks to capture companies in the electric energy infrastructure business including electric grid, electric meters, and energy storage. I wrote previously on GRID two years ago and rated the fund a buy. VOLT seeks to capture companies that will benefit from increased electricity demand including smart grid technology companies. IGF differs from TPZ in that it is a global fund focusing on multiple types of infrastructure including electricity, communication, and transportation.

Funds Compared: Performance, Fees, and Dividend Yields

Because TPZ was initiated in 2024, the fund does not have a lengthy history to compare performance. However, over the past year TPZ has significantly underperformed peer energy funds. For reasons discussed in this article, I believe this trend will continue through 2026 and beyond.

A key drawback for TPZ is its high expense ratio. While the fund offers a significant dividend yield at almost 4%, this yield is eaten away by high fees. Therefore, although the fund has a noteworthy income component, the combination of a high expense ratio with low capital appreciation must also be considered.

Expense Ratio, AUM, and Dividend Yield Comparison

TPZ

GRID

VOLT

IGF

Expense Ratio

0.85%

0.56%

0.75%

0.39%

AUM

$131.05M

$5.68B

$293.50M

$9.11B

Dividend Yield TTM

3.92%

0.97%

0.42%

3.18%

Dividend Growth 3 YR CAGR

N/A

11.97%

N/A

17.41%

Source: Seeking Alpha, 17 Jan 26

TPZ Holdings and Key Consideration Factors

TPZ is dominated by its concentration on midstream energy, or companies that offer services between production and end-use. These midstream companies include Energy Transfer (ET), Etergy Corporation (ETR), and the Williams Companies (WMB). The ETF also is heavy on power generation companies like Constellation Energy (CEG) and NRG Energy (NRG).

Top 10 Holdings for TPZ and Peer Energy Infrastructure Funds

TPZ – 33 holdings

GRID – 113 holdings

VOLT – 32 holdings

IGF – 76 holdings

CEG – 7.04%

SWX:ABBN – 8.15%

POWL – 6.45%

AENA – 5.11%

ET – 6.57%

LON:NG – 8.09%

BELFB – 6.42%

NEE – 5.02%

VST – 5.47%

EPA:SU – 7.78%

NEE – 6.30%

TCL – 4.84%

ETR – 5.42%

ETN – 7.59%

APH – 4.99%

ENB – 3.93%

SRE – 5.42%

JCI – 7.51%

PWR – 4.56%

PAC – 3.87%

TRP – 5.01%

ETR:EOAN – 4.33%

HUBB – 4.20%

IBE – 3.86%

EVRG – 4.93%

BIT:PRY – 4.24%

AEP – 4.05%

AIA – 3.00%

CWEN – 4.91%

PWR – 3.84%

ETR – 3.96%

CEG – 2.90%

NRG – 4.89%

HUBB – 2.96%

ET – 3.94%

SO – 2.87%

WMB – 4.87%

NVT – 2.04%

IDA – 3.81%

WMB – 2.80%

Source: Multiple, compiled by author on 17 Jan 26

Ultimately, TPZ is a highly concentrated fund with almost 60% weight on its top 10 holdings. The fund’s top three holdings alone make up almost 20% weight and are also indicative of TPZ’s active management strategy. While many of the fund’s top holdings offer a strong dividend yield, they also have issues that will lead to limited growth. These factors are discussed below for TPZ’s top three holdings.

Constellation Energy: Supply of Uranium Raises Concern

TPZ’s top holding, CEG, at over 7% weight, represents the first concerning factor for the fund. Constellation Energy’s key competitive advantage is that it provides the largest network of nuclear facilities in the United States. However, Constellation Energy faces unique supply challenges. CEG’s facilities require not just uranium but the conversion of uranium to gas and enriching that gas to fissile U-235 for fuel rods. Currently, the world’s largest producer of uranium is Kazakhstan, a Central Asian nation that has grown closer to China in recent years. Partly because of this supply strain, Constellation Energy has not significantly increased its capacity over the past 8 quarters.

Looking forward, while AI data centers rely on an immense requirement for power, nuclear power provides a minority of this supply. In 2024, natural gas supplied over 40% of the electricity for data centers. Nuclear power, on the other hand, provided only 20%. Given the supply strains for uranium, this disparity will likely persist. Furthermore, CEG’s valuation is currently high with a forward P/E of 33, 70% higher than its sector median. Therefore, even if CEG does see a strong improvement in growth, its valuation leaves little room for a higher share price.

Energy Transfer LP: Lacking Fundamental Growth

The second factor is Energy Transfer, TPZ’s #2 holding, at almost 7% weight. While the energy transportation company may seem bright on the surface, there are issues underneath. Energy Transfer, unlike Constellation Energy, focuses on the transportation of natural gas and makes its profits through fees to transport energy. However, the company has not been able to manage its revenues to drive growth. This problem is exacerbated by a net income margin of just 5.7% along with much of its profits going towards its 7.7% dividend yield.

This lack of profitability and limited growth was further witnessed by the company’s cancellation of the Lake Charles liquified natural gas export facility in Louisiana. Quantifiably, this is also measured in the company’s YoY revenue growth of -4.7%. Overall, ET’s fundamental metrics do not drive a strong holding for TPZ overall. Due to its management of cash flow, Energy Transfer has not shown an ability to see significant growth, despite the strong demand for natural gas.

Vistra Corporation: Concerning Risk Factors Beneath the Surface

The third consideration is Vistra Corporation at about 5.5% weight. While not a significant weight for TPZ, VST is another holding that is representative of the fund’s selection methodology. The Texas-based company provides retail electricity and power generation within the United States and would therefore seem to be a strong income provider. Indeed, in its recent quarterly presentation, Vistra boats strong EBITDA growth and cash generation. However, upon closer examination, we see $18.5B in net debt which has eroded its 30% EBITDA margin down to a 6.7% net income margin. Additionally, its 2.6 ratio of net debt to adjusted EBITDA drives a reliance on pricing power within the electricity market and access to capital.

Looking forward, VST also has an undesirable valuation. With a forward P/E of 26, the company has a much higher valuation than many peer energy producers, adding another concerning factor. The company has also experienced almost a 20% increase in capital expenditures between 2024 and 2025. While this is currently covered by strong cash flow, growing CAPEX increases risk for the company if not sufficiently supported in coming years.

Current Valuation and 2026 Outlook

TPZ’s top three holdings have all experienced negative returns over the past year. This decline has resulted in a flat recent share price return for TPZ as a whole and significant underperformance for the fund compared to peer energy ETFs. Alternatively, VOLT and GRID have both surpassed the S&P 500 Index over the past six months, presenting significant opportunity cost for investment in TPZ.

Despite recent share price underperformance, TPZ’s valuation is not more attractive than peers when comparing its P/E and P/B ratios. Looking forward, TPZ’s valuation and the concerning factors for its top holdings, as previously discussed, signal continued underperformance for the remainder of 2026.

Valuation Metrics for TPZ and Compared Energy Infrastructure Competitors

TPZ

GRID

VOLT

IGF

P/E ratio

20.65

26.72

28.39

20.91

P/B ratio

3.50

3.64

2.42

2.41

Source: Compiled by Author from Multiple Sources, 17 Jan 26

Risks to Investors

Due to its energy concentration, investors in TPZ will experience unique cyclical and policy-specific risks. These factors induce volatility in the price of energy such as natural gas which is used to produce electricity. While TPZ’s performance is not directly tied to price volatility, uncertain prices induce uncertainty for production that is a core part of many TPZ holdings. Just a few years ago, natural gas price volatility was over 90% in certain quarters. Therefore, while residential, commercial, and industrial energy demand will remain persistently high, price volatility will remain a risk factor.

The volatility discussed above, along with the alarming signals for TPZ’s top holdings present risk for investors. Given the muted performance that will likely persist, I see the fund as a sell even for investors seeking income through dividend yields.

Concluding Summary

TPZ is a high-fee fund with limited upside looking forward. While its energy holdings produce a strong dividend yield, investors should not fixate on this one quality. Instead, when examined holistically, TPZ will likely underperform due to a combination of growth, profitability, and valuation issues.

data.title: MSTY Through A Bear Regime: What The First Full Cycle Reveals
data.description:
I maintain a Sell rating on the Yieldmax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY). I have been warning about structural issues in the approach and its implications since March last year, at a time when interest in this high-yield product was picking up. Since August peaks, the interest has been waning a bit, and recent AUM activity suggests investor exhaustion since October.

Other than MSTR's fall since October, nothing has actually changed in the ETF's structure or methodology. This analysis actually dives deeper into MSTY's performance in a first regime since launch when MSTR has really collapsed and finds the performance in line with expectations in such a regime. Investors who flocked to MSTY in mid 2025 were always riding MSTR risks, and if they were indeed bullish on MSTR, then MSTY was not the best tool to monetize it. In fact, MSTY has done relatively well (as is expected) during this fall in MSTR from October 2025.

Since my last coverage, another important development in MSTY is the reverse split that has potentially sent negative signals, along with a crashing share price (in an unsupportive environment). NAV erosion and high yields go hand in hand, and that is a concern, but I am more worried about total return underperformance - that has importantly remained where it was since inception. Unlike in the case of ULTY, where positive changes have emerged in terms of the underlying portfolio selection, option strategy changes, and even some yield moderation, no material changes were visible in MSTY when I checked its holdings and trades. MSTY does not have scope for portfolio level changes like ULTY (given it rides a single stock). But yields and option strategies (like the introduction of net put positions like in ULTY) could have changed - and they have not, as per what a look at the portfolio reveals.

Waning Appetite

The waning appetite phenomenon I talked about at the start is clearly visible in the AUM versus price charts. AUM changes are broadly attributable to inflow, reinvestment of payouts, and price changes, so a clean attribution of waning AUM to either variable is not perfect. But the rise in interest from April 2025 to August is still visible. This is helped by a direct impact of share price stability and its indirect impact on investor interest (high yields on a stable base). The same observations are true for the decline since August, accelerated since October (coincidentally, since my last coverage).

The drop in interest since October follows a similar trail as the price graph. So, that is certainly a factor, mechanically dragging the AUM. However, we can confidently assert that inflows have certainly slowed compared to the earlier regimes, where AUM was growing despite a flat price trajectory.

Yields, NAV Erosion and Reverse Split

The reverse split has grabbed most of the attention in the past few months. Standalone, that is not a negative development in my view. A yield product trading at $100, say, drops to $1 due to heavy yields, may resort to reverse splits for operational reasons or optics. Even an income plan that actually works may do the same. The reverse split per se is not an acknowledgment of issues in a strategy. The core issue is therefore not the yield (and NAV erosion) as well. The core issue is whether reinvestments would have mattered - that is, total returns. If the drop to $1 was accompanied by, say, a payout of $200, reinvesting into a systematically growing total return machine would have been a no-brainer - agnostic of how many units I am forced to hold. I will evaluate the total return performance in a subsequent section.

NAV erosion is an issue for investors not reinvesting payouts. This is because even in a systematically growing strategy, there is less left on the table on the way down (you take the payouts while the underlying is going down) and do not gain as much because of lower stakes on the rebound. As an example, say you invested $100 in MSTR. It travels down to $50. You have taken out $20 systematically as payouts during this fall, reducing the stakes further from $50 to $30. When MSTR rebounds back to $100, i.e., doubles, your investments also double, but only to $60. Adding the $20 already harvested, the net position reads $80, not $100. MSTY, of course, adds an option layer that could plug this $20 hole in the net outcome, but as we will see in the Performance section later (and have seen in my past analyses), the option layer's upside capping mechanism and limited drawdown benefits do not work out very well over full cycles.

One way to address this issue of NAV erosion is to balance yields - particularly if investors intend to use MSTY as a long-term income plan rather than a tactical ploy in flat to pressured MSTR regimes. There, MSTY has not really made any major changes yet to moderate the yield levels. In fact, MSTY's yield levels have only become more aggressive since October.

Portfolio Deep Dive

I downloaded the latest intraday trades and current holdings reported on the MSTY website to understand the strategy's current positioning. The core long position is implemented using options (synthetic position) with positions across February 2026 and March 2026. These are rolled over near or before expiry to maintain the net long effect on MSTR.

Covered calls are written for the near month expiry, as expected, but spread across strikes ranging from $175 to $192.5. The call writing ladder formation ensures some upside capture compared to a simpler call writing at-the-money structure. While some of these strikes can be deemed at-the-money, significant call writing far out of the money shows upside capture is not as restricted as in theory. Another factor that supports upside capture is that the covered call layer accounts for ~70% of the net long position (February and March combined). This balances income positioning with upside retention.

There is a call spread structure also seen, with long positions for the near month expiry for strikes further out - between $182.5 and $200. This layer ensures unlimited upside is captured beyond a point when MSTR rallies, which is great for reducing the upside cap drag. However, I could not see a balanced long exposure here. The call layer is marginally smaller than the short call layer (check the number of share equivalents in the figure above for context) - an attempt to optimize income while retaining upside capture potential. Since the methodology is clearly active, this layer's size could change depending on how management views MSTR in the near month. Since MSTR is down and consolidating now, we could be seeing a bigger upside capture layer now. We may see less long call convexity in another regime.

While the holdings reveal more upside capture possibilities than a theoretical at-the-money covered call strategy (through partial option coverage, and a call spread structure), that also comes with lower income potential or drawdown mitigation possibilities. The funding required for buying the long call layer (in pursuit of upside capture) erodes the option premiums gained from call writing. The balance is so demanding (between income and upside capture), that the ask becomes very high of the active management - and getting the balance right in every market condition is indeed a tougher ask. This was the case in October when I last wrote - and structurally I do not see much difference today in the portfolio. There is no additional long put layer to cushion falls (for example) or material structural changes to address the inherent issues with such a methodology.

This active management requirement shows up in the intraday log I downloaded for 16th January 2026. Assuming it is indicative of how the ETF typically operates, I saw around a 2-3% increase in core exposure in just one day. This could be adjusting to inflow changes, rolling over positions, or speculative positioning—but it shows the amount of activity required even in the core position daily.

On the covered call credit spread layer, I saw over 180k contracts traded. This could also be due to various reasons like a change in outlook or new coverage for added net core exposure or even rollovers. The amount of activity and active calls involved looks very high to me - a tall ask while MSTY tries to manufacture yields every day. This is not necessarily a negative, but introduces more risk of errors in active calls and certainly inflates expenses (close to 1%). Importantly, despite all the activity, it does not create a compelling performance graph over the long term.

Expected Performance Trajectory

As I mentioned before in this analysis (and across older articles), total returns remain my biggest concern when it comes to MSTY. For investments made at inception, MSTY investors are still behind MSTR's total returns - this is now confirmed after MSTR's drawdown had shown more cyclical data across regimes.

The second aspect I wanted to show, now that we have more drawdown data that was missing before, is how MSTY's option layer does not generate meaningful alpha over the long term - something I have been talking about theoretically so far. In a regime spanning from early 2024 to today (just less than 2 years), MSTR has not gone anywhere on an end-to-end basis. MSTY does not meaningfully add any expected alpha from the option layer, nor does it reduce the volatility of the trajectory.

It is actually great that MSTY has not eroded systematically. It has actually captured upsides better (as seen from the portfolio setup analysis) than theory would have suggested. However, that has come at the cost of systematic income accumulation from the option layer over time. MSTY at best becomes a proxy for MSTR, following it closely. But introducing huge active management risks and expensive turnover and forcing a yield, investors in MSTR (aiming for systematic income from the underlying directly) can choose to set.

The AUM pressures have ironically come in a period when MSTY has actually delivered on its promise of reducing drawdowns - it is down by ~32% when MSTR has crashed by ~39%. This is expected behavior, and waning investor behavior in a relatively good period for MSTY shows most investors were actually betting on MSTR rather than MSTY's relative advantages.

Retaining the Sell

My recommendation for MSTY investors remains the same. MSTR-agnostic investors should stay away. Those who believe in MSTR's rebound or long-term strength should invest in MSTR and withdraw income (because that is what MSTY ends up doing anyway). Only for tactical investors, MSTY could make sense - it will cushion drawdowns, although in a limited capacity, and generate some income in flat markets. Looking at the AUM activity, MSTY was certainly not being looked at as a tactical tool - so for a vast majority of MSTY investors, it is a sell.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson: Tempering Expectations After A Massive 2025 (Downgrade)
data.description:
Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) has a full head of steam coming into the Q4 earnings report later this week. Shares are up by more than 50% in the past 12 months, making it among the leading DJIA stocks in the last year. But I'm downgrading shares, purely on valuation.

The chart is magnificent, while earnings growth is steady in the years ahead. Unfortunately, the low-20s P/E is a bit too rich today. The high-quality Health Care name is by no means a sell/short idea, though. With upside growth catalysts on tap and a solid 2.4% dividend yield, the management team continues to deliver. I expect a bottom-line beat on the 21st.

Shares are up 24% just since my Q3 2025 analysis, outperforming the S&P 500 by almost 20 percentage points on a total return basis.

JNJ Trounces XLV and SPY Returns YoY

Back in October, JNJ reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $2.80 topped the Wall Street consensus target of $2.76, while revenue of $24 billion, up 7% from the same period a year earlier, was a material $240 million beat. Moreover, the company raised its FY 2025 sales forecast.

The double-beat and raise was met with muted applause from investors. Shares were precisely unchanged in the session that followed. Looking forward to the January 21 Q4 report, the options market prices in a modest 2.7% earnings-related stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the release. Shares of the $529 billion Health Care large-cap company feature a low 21% implied volatility reading, and short interest on the stock is modest at 0.65%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, JNJ tallied $24 billion in net sales for just the second time in its storied history. Its Innovated Medicine segment grew revenue to $15.6 over the July through September stretch, driven by double-digit gains in 11 of its brands, including DARZALEX, ERLEADA, CARVYKTI, TREMFYA, and SPRAVATO.

Q3 Numbers

Elsewhere, JNJ's MedTech sales hit $8.4 billion, fueled by strong growth in Cardiovascular, particularly Electrophysiology and Abiomed, alongside contributions from Surgery and Vision.

Particularly impressive was the firm's 69.6% gross margin, along with healthy R&D spending that appears to be paying off. From a shareholder perspective, JNJ intends to separate its Orthopaedics business by the end of next year... such a spinoff could create value (as has been the case marketwide this cycle). Recently, there were no immediate volatility catalysts noted at last week's JPMorgan Health Care Conference.

Finally, the management team increased FY 2025 revenue guidance to be in the range of $93.0-$93.4 billion and maintained adjusted operational EPS at $10.63-$10.73.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be about steady in FY 2026 and through the out year. The company could reach $12 in operating per-share earnings on a forward basis by the middle of 2026. But sales growth is only expected to be in the mid-single digits.

Hence, there have been a high 15 sell-side downgrades in the past 12 months, compared to just a pair of upgrades. Still, profitability trends are sublime, with free cash flow per share of $7.76 (about a 3% FCF yield).

JNJ: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, we run into issues. JNJ now trades at more than 21 times expected EPS. That's a near-market multiple and more than a full turn pricier compared to its long-term average. I simply think that's stretched.

If we assume a generous 20x multiple and $11.50 of current-year non-GAAP EPS, then shares should trade near $230. That just doesn't leave much of a margin of safety anymore, particularly considering the 5.6x forward price-to-sales ratio-25% above the long-term average.

JNJ: Above Its LTA P/E

Key risks for JNJ include slower growth in its MedTech area as industry competition rises, lighter revenue gains in new products, and litigation risks that are seemingly always present with the company. On the Q3 earnings call, there were some doubts over JNJ's ability to expand to new markets, though the management team was quick to point out that its pipeline is solid.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, January 21 BMO. Shares trade ex a $1.30 dividend on Tuesday, February 24.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Year Ahead

The Technical Take

With shares close to intrinsic value, JNJ's technical situation is very strong. Notice in the chart below that a powerful uptrend remains intact, dating back to last June. The long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend, while the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has ranged in a bullish zone between 45 and 80. Simply put, the bears have had little chance to pounce on the premium valuation.

Moreover, shares went above and beyond the measured move upside price target of near $210 (blue in the below chart). For now, the rising short-term 50 DMA acts as support, and it has confluence with an uptrend support line. So, $205 (as of this writing) should be your buy point-for those seeking a 'buy the dip' play leading into and after the Q4 earnings report.

JNJ: Support at the 50dma, Bullish RSI Trend, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on JNJ. This DJIA leader and Health Care sector stalwart has returned 50% just since Q2 2025. Shares now appear close to fair value, but the chart is incredibly bullish.

data.title: Small Caps Are Leading The Way
data.description:
I have written articles on this blog over the past year or year and a half about small company stocks beginning to receive investor attention as they began to outperform large company stocks. The small-cap outperformance tended to be short-lived, though, as investors turned their attention back to the so-called Magnificent 7 or 8 mega-cap technology stocks and, more broadly, large company stocks. Of late, though, small-cap stocks are once again leading many other asset classes. As the below table shows and sorted on the 3-month total return column, the leading asset classes are micro-cap, small-cap and mid-cap stocks. The iShares Core S&P Small-Cap ETF (IJR) is up 9.68% versus the S&P 500 Index, up 5.0% over the past three months.

Another small company stock index is the Russell 2000 Index, which is investable by using the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM). This ETF is now outperforming the large-cap S&P 500 ETF (SPY) on a 1-year basis, 19.52% versus 18.14%, respectively. A difference between the S&P 600 Index and the Russell 2000 Index, both focusing on small companies, is the S&P 600 Index requires companies comprising the index to be profitable. However, this profitability requirement is not a metric for the Russell 2000 Index, and a little more than 40% of the companies in this index are unprofitable.

A question investors ask is whether this outperforming trend for small caps is sustainable, and there are some underlying factors that suggest maybe it is. Because a large percentage of small-cap companies are unprofitable, they tend to carry more debt financing on their balance sheet to operate the business. With the Fed lowering interest rates and expected to continue to do so this year, debt financing cost becomes less burdensome for companies using debt. Additionally, as seen in the below table from LSEG I/B/E/S, earnings growth for small companies is expected to be significantly positive through the third quarter of 2027.

Certainly, this outperformance with small-cap stocks might be fleeting; however, some underlying factors like lower interest rates and strong earnings growth seem to suggest otherwise. Up until recently, momentum has favored the Mag 7 or 8 stocks, but as seen in the below chart, all but two are underperforming the S&P 500 Index over the last three months. In short, this rotation into other asset classes would make the equity market rally sustainable through 2026, of course not higher in a straight line.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
